flags.fault_injection: run.sh SIGKILLs llama-server once a slot is processing (~T+110-130 s) and restarts it at ~T+240 s; timestamps recorded in evidence/i3/raw/failover-timeline.json

model.checkpoint: qwen2.5-1.5b-instruct-q4_k_m.gguf (Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct, GGUF Q4_K_M); mock fallback synthesizes deterministic text for the same model id
model.tokenizer: gguf-embedded Qwen2 BPE (llama-server arm); mockengine estimator ~4 bytes/token (fallback window)

hardware.instance_type: local-dev-host (linux/amd64, 4 CPU cores, 15 GiB RAM; llama-server -t 4, mock-backend, infergate, and inferbench co-located on the same cores)

gateway.version: 74f2372 (infergate@74f2372acea62645fa3c1d91689574ea9de7c589, host binary built read-only via git archive; binary sha256 in evidence/i3/logs/build-info.txt)
gateway.config_version: v1-8ac73bb5 (scenarios/b/config/gateway-failover.json: llamacpp primary + openai-type mock fallback, upstream_timeout 300s, stream_write_timeout 30s)

client.location: same host (inferbench on host -> infergate 127.0.0.1:18080 -> llama-server 127.0.0.1:18082 / mock 127.0.0.1:18083, loopback)

hypothesis: Failover demonstration: killing llama-server mid-run (SIGKILL while a slot is processing) terminates ONLY the streams already accepted at kill time, each with exactly one typed upstream_error SSE event (bounded by the in-flight count; never retried after tokens were delivered); every request dispatched while llama-server is down is served by the mock fallback with zero client-visible errors; after llama-server restarts, traffic returns to it automatically (per-request primary-first selection). NOT a latency benchmark: two engines with different timing behavior serve one run.
notes: I3 failover demonstration (mock <-> llama.cpp through the composed path). Latency aggregates over this run mix two engines and must not be quoted; the evidence is the per-segment behavior table in checks.log.
